content.text:
```json
{
  "module": "auth-rbac",
  "scores": {
    "visual_polish": 22,
    "domain_depth": 25,
    "data_richness": 15,
    "interaction_quality": 20,
    "ai_integration": 0,
    "mobile_first": 18,
    "performance": 30,
    "typescript_quality": 35,
    "error_handling": 22,
    "realtime": 10,
    "security": 28,
    "differentiation": 15,
    "enterprise_readiness": 20
  },
  "overall_score": 21,
  "issues": [
    {
      "id": "auth-rbac-C3-001",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "security",
      "title": "ProtectedRoute renders children before permission check completes",
      "file": "src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx",
      "description": "The ProtectedRoute component checks `isDevBypassActive()` and renders children immediately without waiting for useAuth loading state to resolve. When Supabase is configured but session fetch is in flight, there is a window where unauthenticated users see protected content. The loading skeleton only shows when `loading` is true AND dev bypass is false, but the permission check via `requiredPermission`/`moduleId` runs against stale/empty permission state during the loading window. The component must block rendering until both auth AND permissions loading are complete.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx, in the default exported component: BEFORE: The component checks `isDevBypassActive()` early and may render children before `useAuth().loading` and `usePermissions().loading` both resolve to false. When `loading` is true from useAuth, the skeleton shows, but when usePermissions is still loading separately, the permission check runs against default/empty state, potentially allowing a brief flash of protected content or incorrectly denying access. AFTER: Add an explicit combined loading guard at the top of the render logic: `if (authLoading || permissionsLoading) return <SkeletonLoader ariaLabel=\"Verifying access\" />;` before ANY permission or auth checks. Destructure `loading` from both `useAuth()` (rename to `authLoading`) and `usePermissions()` (rename to `permissionsLoading`). This ensures zero content flash during session hydration. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "auth-rbac-C3-002",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "error_handling",
      "title": "Login page missing error message mapping for Supabase auth errors",
      "file": "src/pages/Login.tsx",
      "description": "Per PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA AUTH-007, raw Supabase error strings like 'Invalid login credentials' or 'AuthApiError' must never be displayed to users. The Login page currently passes error messages from useAuth directly to the UI without mapping them through a user-friendly error mapper function. This violates acceptance criteria and exposes internal implementation details to end users.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Login.tsx, in the Login component: BEFORE: Error messages from the auth hook (e.g. `error` state from useAuth) are rendered directly in the UI without transformation, showing raw Supabase strings like 'Invalid login credentials' or 'Email not confirmed'. AFTER: Add a `mapAuthError` function at the top of the file that maps known Supabase error messages to user-friendly strings: 'Invalid login credentials' or 'invalid_credentials' → 'Email or password is incorrect', messages containing 'email not confirmed' → 'Please check your email to confirm your account', messages containing 'rate limit' or 'too many' → 'Too many attempts. Please try again in a few minutes', messages containing 'fetch' or 'network' or 'failed' → 'Unable to connect. Check your internet connection', messages containing 'already registered' → 'An account with this email already exists', and pass through any unrecognized messages unchanged. Wrap every rendered error string with this mapper. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "auth-rbac-C3-003",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "accessibility",
      "title": "RequestAccessPage in PermissionGate is incomplete — missing closing JSX",
      "file": "src/components/auth/PermissionGate.tsx",
      "description": "The RequestAccessPage component's JSX is truncated in the source. The div with `style={{ maxWidth: 480, margin: 'auto', padding: 48, textAlign: 'center' }}` has no closing tag or children rendered. This will cause a TypeScript/JSX compilation error or render an empty page when users without permission are shown the access denied view. Per acceptance criteria, this must show the role name, a clear explanation, and a 'Request Access' action button.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/components/auth/PermissionGate.tsx, in the RequestAccessPage component: BEFORE: The component's JSX is truncated after the opening `<div>` tag — it contains no content, no heading, no explanation text, no action button, and no proper closing tags. This renders a blank page for unauthorized users. AFTER: Complete the RequestAccessPage component with: (1) A ShieldAlert icon (already imported) rendered at 48px in textTertiary color, (2) An h2 heading 'Access Restricted' using typography.fontSize.xl and textPrimary color, (3) A paragraph explaining 'You do not have permission to view {moduleName || \"this page\"}. Your current role is {role || \"unknown\"}.' in textSecondary color, (4) A button labeled 'Go to Dashboard' styled with the primary orange color (colors.primary) as background and white text, border-radius borderRadius.md, padding spacing[2] spacing[4], cursor pointer, that calls navigate('/dashboard') on click, (5) Proper closing tags for all elements. Add role='alert' and aria-live='polite' to the outer div. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "auth-rbac-C3-004",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "interaction_quality",
      "title": "No loading state on login submit button — allows double submission",
      "file": "src/pages/Login.tsx",
      "description": "Per acceptance criteria AUTH-001, the login form must show a loading state during authentication. Currently the submit button has no disabled state during the auth request, no spinner, and no visual feedback that the request is in flight. Users can click multiple times causing duplicate auth requests.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Login.tsx, in the Login component's form submit button: BEFORE: The sign-in button does not disable itself or show a loading indicator while the authentication request is in progress. The `loading` state from useAuth is not applied to the button's `disabled` prop or used to swap the button text to a spinner. AFTER: (1) Set the submit button's `disabled` prop to the `loading` boolean from useAuth, (2) When `loading` is true, replace the button text with 'Signing in...' and add `opacity: 0.7` and `cursor: 'not-allowed'` to the button style, (3) Add `aria-busy={loading}` to the button element so screen readers announce the loading state. Ensure the button maintains its original width during loading to prevent layout shift. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "auth-rbac-C3-005",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "mobile_first",
      "title": "ProtectedRoute skeleton loader sidebar shows on mobile, breaking layout",
      "file": "src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx",
      "description": "The SkeletonLoader renders a 220px sidebar placeholder on desktop but the media query check uses `useState(() => window.innerWidth < 768)` which fails during SSR and only updates on resize events. On initial mobile load, `isMobile` defaults based on the initial window width which may be incorrect during hydration. The sidebar skeleton should use CSS-based responsive hiding rather than JS state to prevent flash of desktop layout on mobile.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx, in the SkeletonLoader component: BEFORE: The sidebar visibility is controlled by a `useState` + `useEffect` resize listener pattern. On initial render, `isMobile` is set from `window.innerWidth < 768` which can be incorrect during hydration or in test environments where window is mocked. The resize listener adds unnecessary JS overhead for a simple responsive hide. AFTER: Remove the `isMobile` state and resize event listener entirely. Instead, add a `@media` style approach: render the sidebar div always but give it an additional inline style property using a CSS class or a wrapper div. Since we cannot use CSS modules (per codebase rules), use a simpler approach: add `display: 'none'` via a style object for the sidebar and wrap it in a div that uses `@media` via a `<style>` tag injected into the component, OR more practically, keep the JS approach but use `matchMedia` listener instead of resize event for better performance: `const mql = window.matchMedia('(max-width: 767px)'); setIsMobile(mql.matches); mql.addEventListener('change', e => setIsMobile(e.matches)); return () => mql.removeEventListener('change', ...)`. This is more reliable than resize events. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    }
  ],
  "invented_features": [],
  "competitive_intel": "Procore's auth is enterprise-grade with SSO (SAML/OIDC), scoped API tokens per project, and granular role-based permissions enforced server-side with audit logging. Their mobile app uses biometric auth for quick re-entry. Fieldwire supports magic link login for field crews who forget passwords. SiteSync's auth module has the right architecture (Supabase Auth + RLS + PermissionGate) but the implementation has critical gaps: incomplete UI components, missing error mapping, and loading race conditions that would fail a security review.",
  "summary": "The auth-rbac module has correct architectural patterns (ProtectedRoute, PermissionGate, usePermissions hook, RLS-backed tenantGuard) but the implementation is incomplete and regression-prone. The RequestAccessPage component is truncated and renders nothing, the login page exposes raw Supabase error strings, and the ProtectedRoute has a race condition where content can flash before permissions resolve. The score dropped from 32 to 26 likely because the RequestAccessPage truncation was introduced or exposed by a prior fix attempt. The five issues target: (1) preventing unauthorized content flash during auth loading, (2) mapping auth errors to user-friendly messages per acceptance criteria AUTH-007, (3) completing the truncated RequestAccessPage component, (4) adding loading state to the login button to prevent double-submission, and (5) fixing the mobile skeleton layout. All five are surgical single-file fixes that address P0 security and P1 UX requirements from the acceptance criteria."
}
```
